narrative: I'm sorry to share that our admissions model predicted a 69.8% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application to our law school was rejected—it's designed to prioritize students likely to succeed on that critical test. Your profile shows you ranked in the 2nd decile for Year 1 grades and a very low 1st decile for Year 3, both well below the typical student's mid-range performance around the 5th or 6th decile. Your LSAT score of 29.5 and undergraduate GPA of 2.4 are also noticeably lower than the averages of about 37 and 3.2 seen in successful applicants. As a male Native American student from an upper-middle-income family planning full-time studies, you're in line with some common demographics, like the majority full-time and male students, but your racial background is less represented at just under 2% of the dataset. When the model evaluated you, your Year 3 decile rank stood out as the most influential factor, strongly pushing the prediction toward expecting a bar failure. Right behind it, your LSAT score had the next biggest impact in that direction, followed closely by your Year 1 decile rank. Your family income level, racial background, and undergraduate GPA each played supporting roles in tipping the scales toward failure, while factors like being full-time and male had minimal sway. Overall, these academic markers painted a picture riskier than the typical applicant, who tends to have stronger high school and undergrad records aligning with bar passage. It's tough to hear, but this highlights where your strengths and gaps lie compared to peers who passed. Looking ahead, you could realistically boost your odds by retaking the LSAT to aim closer to 37 or higher, as it's a key changeable factor. Strengthening any future GPA or equivalent through targeted prep courses might help too. Most importantly, dedicate time now to bar exam-specific study habits, like practice tests and weak-area drills, since your past deciles suggest room for better performance under pressure. With focused effort on these, you'd stand a much stronger chance next time around. Keep pushing—you've got potential to turn this around.
